titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to Frances Anne Edgeworth, May 19, 1819
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Wake Forest University, University of Tennessee, Xavier University, Texas A&M University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

editionStmt.edition: First digital edition in TEI, date: January 14, 2026

availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of Bodleian Library
availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to Frances Anne Edgeworth,

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

div.p:
How melancholy how dreadful
my dearest mother is the account
of all you have gone through this
last fortnight. Will it be any com.
fort to you or will it add to your
pain to know how much I pity
you and how excessively anxious
we are and must be about you &
-about you all. I have no doubt
that every possible prudent pre-
caution has been taken by you &
dear Lovell but - it is a most
horrible situation & poor Lucy!
What accumulated evils poured
upon you all at once & just at
the moment when we flattered
ourselves that you were all most
happy enjoying Lovells well de-
served praises & gallant Sheriff's
show. I have rec.d yours of the 7.ththe 13.th &the 9.th - in this order . One letter of yours, the
first you wrote giving an account
(I suppose) of poor Mary Anne Fox's
danger has never yet reached us
- I conclude it came to Lord Carringtons
the day we left him & that he forwarded
it as he says he forwarded all letters
for us to Knutsford. We shall find
it there on our arrival. We leave
this place tomorrow morning 11 o clock
- shall be at Mr . & Mrs Wedgwoods to
dinner. They no longer live at Etruria
they have just changed to Maer
near about 8 or 9 miles from Woore their
post town. We shall stay with them
till saturday . drink tea on saturday
with Mr . & Mrs . Holland and spend
4 days with them & 4 with Mrs . M Proys
- Then pursue our journey to Holyhead
which we hope to reach by 29th . or 30th.
- All this I have told you I believe before
but I repeat to make sure of there
being no mistakes on my part. I
will write to Mrs Waller from Knutsford.
think of going round by Black Castle
in my way home to spend one day
with my dear aunt; because from
the time I arrive at home I shall
never stir or think of seeing her
till I have completed my manu-
script & completed the correction
of the proof sheets which cannot be till
Xmass. One day would settle her
mind & mine & I hope you will ap-
prove of this but I shall not mention
209 it to my aunt till I have your
answer dearest Mother & I entreat
you will say exactly what you wish

God knows I wish I was with you
this moment & regret heartily the
cursed engagements which will de
lay us this fortnight. I made only
these Knutsford engagements when
I first came to England & they have
hampered us ever since. When
shall I learn sense enough never
to make any promises - ?

And now dear mother what
will you think about Honora's
determination to stay with her
aunt. I thought I was quite sure
that you would approve of it and
I) consider it as I do the very best
thing on every account that c.d be
done: but one line in the last
letter of your of of the 9th . which I did not receive
till yesterday evening, throws me in
to anxiety. You say you have a latent
fear now of dear Honora's offering
to stay with her aunts". Now it had
all been settled before your letter came
& I never have shewn this line to any
one but Fanny because as she agrees with
me in thinking it would only give
pain & could not now alter any thing -
- My aunts were very unwilling to
let Honora stay urging that it would
be very dull for her & too great a sacri
fice &c but she urged it so much & I
added in private converse with
them that I dreaded much the effect
upon Honora's mind of returning to Edgeworth's Town . Fanny joined me in
this fear & told me that you had the same

I hope my dearest mother I have
not done wrong for I certainly did use
my utmost power influence to pre-
vail on my aunts to accept her offer
- I further thought it would look well
for Mr . Mrs . Sneyd that she shd . stay - for I
know much has been said by that
World which perhaps is not worth caring
for but for which they do care.
Mrs . Sneyd in a letter to Honora distinctly
says "she can have no objection to her
staying with her aunts"
In a previous letter to me this indeed
was limitted by the words while we
are absent. But as the limitation
was omitted or withdrawn in Mrs . S's
letter to Ho. she acted upon the letter
of the law . We are all gasping with im-
patience to know what she will
say in reply to the letter sent on monday announcing Honora's
210 & my aunts determination that she shd . stay. I am clear
Mrs . Sneyd has no more expectation of it than of the Persian
ambassador's being here. We depend upon Francis Beaufort
for bringing her back to Ireland about August & perhaps
bringing little Francis with them. Francis B. had promised
that at any time he wld . undertake her convoy & therefore
begged that the difficulty of transport might be no obstacle to
any wish she might have to stay in England. - You may know
by your own feelings what an agony I shall be in till I
hear from you again & find that you approve- - I have
promised her that if Mrs . Sneyds answer does not come
to daytill after I am gone & that if it does not accept properly I will return
for her at her or meet her at some appointed place - I
will do any anything in short that is most for her happiness
- I know I personally sacrifice a great deal in giving
up her company on my return home - especially
in her sympathy & assistance in finishing that manu-
script every bit & line of which she has gone over with
me & knows better than any one living. I know well
too how much Fanny gives up - And what a groaning
poor [damage]Ha[unclear]rri[/unclear]et[/damage] will make when she hears Honora is
not [damage]coming[/coming] - I see her raging about the room. But still
if it is for Honora's happiness & for the happiness of these
two dear excellent aunts surely you will all be satisfied.

Poor Lucy!- I have never got the image of that
poor creatures sufferings out of my head since I read last night your des
cription of them : How fortunate it was that Gardiner
was therewith you & that you consulted him : . There is hope that
being taken in time all may be set to rights. - & without
injury to her shape . . I long to be with you more than
pen or words can express.- Lady Granards note and offer was truly
kind -

Thank God, Fanny is well. There is that
comfort for you, that delight in life . She is in much
better health and looks that when she came to England
and her mind is certainly satisfied & at ease - And
you will have her dear mother very soon - How
grateful I am to you for all the pleasure & pride
I have felt in her - perhaps you will never know
because I can never fully say -

Your letter about India is admirable - because
it entirely agrees in my own opinion - -both about as to
India & about the acceptance of pecuniary favor
- I may venture to say this now; as I see how you
will decide - Lovells simile - accepting the ladder
& refusing the rungs is very witty & apposite. I wd . there
-fore refuse ladder & rungs - upon the very reasons
you & Lovell have stated - and all which my father
would have stated in the same circumstances -

I will write to Lord C your thanks in your own
words - and beg things may remain as they are
- What you say about W. & the tenaciousness he
would feel about his guardianship had occurred to me
- and is of consequence - The sense of dependance in
dear little Francis's own mind too would be a strong objection
- in short - as there is no change to be made in the education imme
diately you are right to leave things as they are - but I have
no hesitation in deciding my own opinion that the offer
should not be accepted for Francis. - Taking in all his
chances which Lovell so generously states - But which
I sincerely hope may be put off by Lovells marrying & having a representative
of his own - worthy of him, as he is of his father.

Tell that dear brother this with my most affectionate
love & high esteem - and now my eyeballs ache so
I must have done. Thank you dearest friend for
being whether in grief or joy ever the same kindest
of friends & mothers to your grateful daughter in law
Thank God!
Maria Edgeworth

My aunts are wonderfully well & much the better
I think for having much more to do & more motive
for exertion & more sense of being of use & substantive use
1819 no hesitation in deciding Mrs . Edgeworth.
